title: Bart Gets Famous

jokes.text: "Today will be a day like every other day." D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer reads his horoscope expecting something meaningful
jokes.punchline: Gets the most mundane prediction possible, followed by his signature frustrated exclamation
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer character moment - his eternal optimism crushed by mundane reality

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's see what the stars have in store for me today...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You will eat food and then become hungry again.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Jawohl, mein Mommandant.
jokes.setup: Marge asks Bart not to whistle
jokes.punchline: Bart responds with Nazi-style obedience but calls her 'Mommandant' (Mom + Commandant)
jokes.explanation: Smart wordplay that captures Bart's rebellious nature while being clever rather than just snotty

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, please stop whistling.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yes, Mommandant!

jokes.text: The only one who has it easy is Marge. [Marge grunting with effort]
jokes.setup: Homer claims Marge has it easy while everyone else works
jokes.punchline: Cut immediately to Marge doing strenuous physical labor
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual contradiction of Homer's oblivious statement - classic sitcom irony executed well

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You have it easy while everyone else works.

jokes.text: Ah, Lise, won't it be great to cast off the shackles of the soul-crushing hellhole that is Springfield Elementary? Oh! I'm sorry. I forgot. Your class isn't going.
jokes.setup: Bart enthusiastically describes escaping school to Lisa
jokes.punchline: Realizes mid-speech that Lisa isn't going and has to stay in the 'soul-crushing hellhole'
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Bart's lack of awareness followed by awkward realization

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa, dude, I'm totally cutting school today. I'm gonna go to the arcade, catch some rays, maybe catch a matinee...

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, I'm going to school.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What? But Lisa, we could be free! We could escape this soul-crushing hellhole!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I have perfect attendance and I'm not about to break it.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Aw, man. So you're really staying?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, I guess I'm stuck here too then. Great. Back to the soul-crushing hellhole.

jokes.text: You're right, Bart. School is for losers.
jokes.setup: Lisa, the studious overachiever, responds to being left out of the field trip
jokes.punchline: Agrees that school is for losers, completely against her character
jokes.explanation: Lisa saying the opposite of what she believes shows her dejection in a character-driven way

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: School is for losers.

jokes.text: And that's how I cured all disease, ended war and reunited the entire cast of TV's Facts of Life including longtime holdout Tootie.
jokes.setup: Lisa fantasizes about her future accomplishments
jokes.punchline: Escalates from noble achievements to reuniting a sitcom cast, treating Tootie as equally important
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that deflates serious achievements with pop culture absurdity - 'longtime holdout Tootie' is perfect

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'll win the Nobel Prize in Literature, become Secretary of State, and broker peace in the Middle East.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Then I'll reunite the cast of The Facts of Life, and get Tootie back together with everyone.

jokes.text: Hush, field trip boy! Impaled on my Nobel Peace Prize. How ironic.
jokes.setup: Lisa continues her fantasy while Bart interrupts
jokes.punchline: Lisa imagines dying violently on her Peace Prize and notes the irony
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that's perfectly in character for Lisa - intellectual enough to appreciate the irony of her own violent death

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: And then I'd die in a freak accident while accepting my Nobel Peace Prize, which is pretty ironic when you think about it.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa, you're such a dork.

jokes.text: Oh! Not the box factory again, Seymour.
jokes.setup: Principal Skinner is about to announce the field trip destination
jokes.punchline: Edna immediately recognizes it's the same boring box factory they always visit
jokes.explanation: Establishes the recurring nature of this disappointing field trip choice

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Class, I'm pleased to announce this year's field trip destination.

dialogue.speaker: Edna
dialogue.line: Let me guess—the box factory?

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: How did you know?

dialogue.speaker: Edna
dialogue.line: Because it's always the box factory, Seymour. Always.

jokes.text: Class, instead of going to the box factory today we'll be going to the... box factory.
jokes.setup: Skinner builds suspense like he's announcing a change of plans
jokes.punchline: Reveals they're going exactly where everyone expected
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection structure that perfectly captures institutional monotony

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Children, I have an announcement. Due to unexpected circumstances, we will not be going to the Springfield Museum of Natural History today.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Awww!

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Instead, we will be going to... the Springfield Museum of Natural History.

jokes.text: Damn TV! You've ruined my imagination just like you've ruined my ability to-- to, um-- uh-- Oh, well.
jokes.setup: Bart tries to escape into fantasy like Lisa but can't think of anything
jokes.punchline: Blames TV for ruining his imagination, then can't even finish the thought, proving the point
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary where the joke structure perfectly demonstrates its own point - Bart literally can't complete his criticism of TV because TV has affected his attention span

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Man, I can't think of anything. My imagination is like, totally dead.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It's all because of TV. TV has ruined my...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh... what was I saying?

jokes.text: Do any of these boxes have candy in 'em? No. Will they ever? No. We only make boxes to ship nails.
jokes.setup: Bart asks hopefully about candy in boxes
jokes.punchline: The most disappointing possible answer - boxes for nails, the opposite of candy
jokes.explanation: Sharp contrast between childish hope and industrial reality

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Do these boxes have candy in them?

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: No, they're boxes of nails.

jokes.text: When will we be able to see a finished box, sir? Oh, we don't assemble them here. That's done in Flint, Michigan.
jokes.setup: Student asks to see the one interesting thing - a completed product
jokes.punchline: Even that mundane satisfaction is denied - they don't even make complete boxes
jokes.explanation: Perfect example of how modern manufacturing splits processes to make everything more boring

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Can we see the one interesting thing - a completed product?

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Even that mundane satisfaction is denied - they don't even make complete boxes.

jokes.text: Any of your workers had their hands cut off by machinery? No. And then the hand started crawling around and tried to strangle everybody? No, that has never happened. Any popped eyeballs?
jokes.setup: Bart asks about workplace injuries
jokes.punchline: Escalates to increasingly graphic and absurd horror movie scenarios
jokes.explanation: Bart's morbid curiosity escalating to B-movie horror is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So what kind of workplace injuries happen here?

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Well, we have the occasional paper cut, maybe someone slips on the stairs...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What about something more serious?

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Oh, well there was that incident where someone got their tie caught in the copy machine...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, but like, what if someone's head got stuck in there?

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Uh... I suppose that could happen.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: And then the machine just kept copying, over and over, pressing harder and harder until—

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: OK, I think we should move on to the next department.

jokes.text: This room is the most popular part of our tour. It's just like the other rooms. Yes, but with one important difference. Oh. We took that out. Yes, it is just like the other rooms.
jokes.setup: Guide builds up this room as special and different
jokes.punchline: Admits they removed whatever made it different, so it's identical to everything else
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of expectations - builds up something special then immediately destroys it

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: And here we have our most special room, completely unique and unlike anything else in the facility...

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Of course, we had to remove whatever made it special and different, so now it's identical to everything else.

jokes.text: What's that building over there? That's just a TV studio where they film Krusty the Clown and other non-box-related programs. Since it has nothing to do with boxes, I'll just shut these blinds.
jokes.setup: Student spots something actually interesting - a TV studio
jokes.punchline: Guide dismisses it and literally blocks their view because it's not about boxes
jokes.explanation: Sets up Bart's eventual escape while showing the guide's rigid focus on boxes above all else

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Look! A TV studio!

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Hmm? Oh, no, no, no. That's not part of the tour. Now, if you'll direct your attention back here to these boxes...

jokes.text: Now, here's my office. If you'll direct your eyes to the floor, you'll see a yellow line. Follow it. It will lead you around my desk and back out the door.
jokes.setup: Guide shows them his office as if it's special
jokes.punchline: Immediately directs them to follow a line that just leads them right back out
jokes.explanation: Absurd bureaucratic tour that gives the appearance of showing something while showing nothing

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: And here's my office! As you can see, it's quite spacious.

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Now if you'll follow me, this way please!

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Right out the exit. Thank you for visiting!

jokes.text: I gotta get out of here. Aha! The perfect escape.
jokes.setup: Bart can't take the boring tour anymore
jokes.punchline: Visual of Bart finding his escape route to the TV studio
jokes.explanation: Functional plot advancement with Bart's characteristic impulsiveness

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I can't take this boring tour anymore!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait... I found my escape route to the TV studio!

jokes.text: Simpson, I'm giving you until the count of three to come out. One! Two! Three! I've done all I can do.
jokes.setup: Skinner gives Bart an ultimatum with counting
jokes.punchline: Immediately gives up after counting, claiming he's exhausted all options
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner - bureaucratic procedures over actual problem-solving

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Bart, I'm giving you to the count of three.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: One... two... three. Well, I've exhausted all my options.

jokes.text: Y'ello? You'll have to speak up. I'm wearing a towel.
jokes.setup: Homer answers phone normally
jokes.punchline: Claims he can't hear because he's wearing a towel, as if that affects hearing
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer logic that's both completely wrong and somehow endearing - became a widely quoted line

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hello?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I can't hear you, I'm wearing a towel!

jokes.text: Ay, ay, ay! No me gusta! I'm sorry. I'm really not comfortable with this, Ethan. It's just-- It's the same, old, tired gags, isn't it? I mean, let's give the audience some credit. How about a giant mousetrap? I love it!
jokes.setup: Krusty complains about tired, old comedy gags
jokes.punchline: Immediately loves the suggestion of an equally tired gag - a giant mousetrap
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-commentary on comedy itself - Krusty's pretensions immediately collapse

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I'm so sick of tired, old comedy gags. We need something fresh, something new!

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: What about a giant mousetrap?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: A giant mousetrap? That's genius! Pure genius!

jokes.text: He might have fallen into one of these machines! Oh, my God! That's his lucky red hat! He's a box! My boy's a box! Damn you! A box!
jokes.setup: Homer fears Bart fell into box-making machinery
jokes.punchline: Believes Bart has literally become a box, mourns dramatically
jokes.explanation: Homer's leap to the most dramatic possible conclusion is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No! Bart! Oh God, he's been turned into a box!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My boy... my precious boy... reduced to corrugated cardboard.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why? Why would you take him from me like this?

jokes.text: Tragic news tonight. 120 dead in a tidal wave in Kuala La-la-- Pur-- Kuala Lum-pur-- France.
jokes.setup: News anchor tries to pronounce foreign place name
jokes.punchline: Gives up and just says 'France' instead of Kuala Lumpur
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Kent's incompetence disguised as professionalism

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We go now live to our correspondent in... in... Kwal-a... Kwalu... Kwa... France.

jokes.text: Bart questions 'Yoink?' - confusion at Kent's made-up exclamation
jokes.setup: Kent uses nonsense word 'yoink'
jokes.punchline: Bart's confused reaction to the absurd exclamation
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction joke that highlights the absurdity of Kent's behavior

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yoink? What the—

jokes.text: I'm Bart Simpson. I saved you from jail. Oh, I-- I reunited you with your estranged father. Uh, I don't, uh-- I saved your career, man! Remember your comeback special? Yeah, well, what have you done for me lately?
jokes.setup: Bart lists all his past favors for Krusty from previous episodes
jokes.punchline: Krusty dismisses everything with 'what have you done for me lately?'
jokes.explanation: Great callback to series history while perfectly capturing Krusty's selfish nature

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Remember when I saved your career? When I got you that spot on the Late Show? When I helped you with your comeback?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: What have you done for me lately?

jokes.text: I got you that Danish. And I'll never forget it.
jokes.setup: Bart mentions the tiny favor of getting a Danish
jokes.punchline: Krusty treats this minor act like it's more important than saving his career
jokes.explanation: Perfect reversal showing how Krusty values immediate gratification over past loyalty

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Krusty, could you do me a tiny favor? Can you get me a Danish?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: A Danish? Kid, you're asking me to risk my entire career! Do you know what I've done for you?

jokes.text: Wow! A big clown hankie. It's a towel, you yutz! I want you to wash it. You're my new assistant.
jokes.setup: Bart excitedly thinks he's getting a special clown accessory
jokes.punchline: It's just a dirty towel he has to wash as menial labor
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Bart's excitement about show business glamour

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, a special clown accessory? For me?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah, kid. Here ya go.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: A dirty towel. Now get scrubbin'.

jokes.text: A powerful tidal wave in Kuala Lumpur has killed 120 people. Ay, chihuahua! Whoa, whoa, whoa!
jokes.setup: Serious news about deadly tragedy
jokes.punchline: Bumblebee Man's inappropriate cartoon reaction to real death
jokes.explanation: Dark humor contrasting serious news with cartoon character reactions

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We interrupt this program to bring you serious news of a deadly tragedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bumblebee Man
dialogue.line: ¡Ay caramba! *makes exaggerated cartoon sound effects and ridiculous physical comedy*

jokes.text: I've got a weekend job helping the poor, and I'm only eight. That's not a job. It's a waste of time. What can poor people pay you? Nothin'! What satisfaction do you get from helping them? None! Who wants to help poor people anyway? Nobody!
jokes.setup: Lisa mentions her volunteer work
jokes.punchline: Bart's cynical deconstruction of charity work using purely selfish logic
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Bart's amoral worldview while highlighting Lisa's idealism

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I've been doing volunteer work at the animal shelter.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Let me guess — you feel all warm and fuzzy inside, like you're making a real difference, right? But really you're just doing it so you can feel superior to everyone else and tell people about it at school so they think you're some kind of saint.

jokes.text: Why not? I remember my first after-school job. I was in a band. Hello, everybody. I'm Archie Bell, and I'm also The Drells.
jokes.setup: Homer reminisces about his first job in a band
jokes.punchline: Quotes someone else's song introduction as if it were his own experience
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer confused memory - thinks he was someone else entirely

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I remember my first gig with the band. We were so nervous, but then I thought, 'Come together, right now, over me.' Those were the days!

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Homer, that's the Beatles.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know! I was there!

jokes.text: Hey, whatsa matter, you? You crazy kid, you chasing away my business. Buzz off, Giuseppe. Pepe, go for the face!
jokes.setup: Giuseppe confronts Homer about his music
jokes.punchline: Escalates to ordering violence against a customer
jokes.explanation: Standard escalation to cartoon violence, less creative than other moments

dialogue.speaker: Giuseppe
dialogue.line: Your music is too loud!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I can't hear you over my music!

dialogue.speaker: Giuseppe
dialogue.line: Turn it down or I'll throw you out!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You and what army?

dialogue.speaker: Giuseppe
dialogue.line: That's it! Bring me my shotgun. I'm gonna kill this guy!

jokes.text: Dad, I'm asking if I can get a job. Gig, Son. When you're a musician, a job is called a gig.
jokes.setup: Bart asks about getting a job
jokes.punchline: Homer corrects his terminology while ignoring the actual question
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's tendency to focus on irrelevant details

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, how do I get a job?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well son, first of all it's called 'work', not a 'job'.

jokes.text: Don't listen to him, kid. This is a dream factory. The birthplace of magic and enchantment. Now I need you to go clean out my toilet.
jokes.setup: Krusty gives inspirational speech about show business magic
jokes.punchline: Immediately assigns the most unglamorous task possible
jokes.explanation: Sharp contrast between romantic ideals and mundane reality of the industry

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Listen kid, in show business, there's a magic to it all. The lights, the audience, the applause - that's what it's all about!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Now get in the back and unclog the toilet.

jokes.text: Right in here, boy. If it-- Ooh! I don't know what I was thinking last night. This'll take you a couple hours.
jokes.setup: Krusty shows Bart the toilet to clean
jokes.punchline: Visual reveals such a disgusting state that even Krusty is shocked
jokes.explanation: Gross-out humor that reinforces Krusty's disgusting lifestyle

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Here's the toilet you gotta clean, kid.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: [looks inside and recoils in horror]

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: What? What is it? [peers in] Oh my God! Even I'm grossed out!

jokes.text: I'm tellin' ya. I do work on the Krusty Show. Look at the credits. There's my name, right there-- Bart Simpson. Looks more like 'Brad Storch.' No, it says, 'Betty.' 'Betty Symington.'
jokes.setup: Bart tries to prove he works on TV by showing the credits
jokes.punchline: His name is so mangled in credits that classmates read completely different names
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how industry credits don't actually give recognition

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Check it out, man. My name's right there in the credits.

dialogue.speaker: Classmate 1
dialogue.line: Barclay Simpson?

dialogue.speaker: Classmate 2
dialogue.line: I'm reading Bart Sampson.

dialogue.speaker: Classmate 3
dialogue.line: That says Bert Simpleton.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Aw, man!

jokes.text: It's okay, Son. Who cares what a bunch of fourth-graders think? You're doing what you want to do with your life. Nothin' else matters. Thanks, Dad. That's great advice. Yep. Well, that's what got me where I am today. Oh.
jokes.setup: Homer gives inspiring life advice about following your dreams
jokes.punchline: Realizes his own life contradicts his advice, deflating the moment
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment where Homer's wisdom is undercut by self-awareness of his own failures

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Follow your dreams, boy. Work hard, stay focused, and you can be anything you want.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Really, Dad?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure! Look at me. I set my sights high and... and... well, I'm... I work at a nuclear power plant.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's not really inspiring.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Shut up, boy.

jokes.text: Yes, Krusty? Bart, I need to get your fingerprints on a candlestick. Meet me in the conservatory chop-chop.
jokes.setup: Krusty calls Bart during class
jokes.punchline: Casually asks Bart to help frame someone for murder using Clue references
jokes.explanation: Dark humor mixing murder conspiracy with board game references - perfectly captures Krusty's criminality

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey kid, it's Krusty. Listen, I need you to help me frame someone for murder.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, Mr. Krusty, I'm in class right now.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: So? It was Colonel Mustard in the library with the candlestick. You gotta plant evidence in the conservatory.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: This is insane!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: What's insane is you not helping me! Miss Scarlet's got an alibi, so we're gonna pin it on Mr. Green. Now get movin'!

jokes.text: Aah! There's cheese in this sandwich! Surely you know I'm lactose intolerant! Sorry. Sorry? Do you know how sick this is going to make me? Oh, boy. Come stand next to the bathroom door. I want to yell at you some more.
jokes.setup: Krusty discovers cheese in his sandwich
jokes.punchline: Plans to continue yelling at assistant while dealing with digestive consequences
jokes.explanation: Shows Krusty's petty tyranny and attention to personal comfort over everything else

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey, there's cheese in this sandwich! I didn't order cheese!

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Sorry boss, I thought you'd like it.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Well you thought wrong! Now I'm gonna have to yell at you AND deal with the heartburn later!

jokes.text: Bart! I need to use you in a sketch. You want me to be on the show? It's just one line. Mel's supposed to say it, but he's dead. Dead? Or sick. I don't know. I forget.
jokes.setup: Krusty offers Bart a chance to be on TV
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions Mel might be dead, then admits he doesn't know or care
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty - treats potentially tragic news with complete indifference

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey kid, you wanna be on TV? I got an opening for a new sidekick.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Really? That'd be great!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah, ever since Mel died. Or maybe he didn't die. I dunno, I don't really keep track of these things.

jokes.text: All you gotta do is say, 'I am waiting for a bus.' Then I hit you with pies for five minutes. Got that?
jokes.setup: Krusty explains Bart's simple role
jokes.punchline: The 'sketch' is just extended pie-throwing violence with no comedy structure
jokes.explanation: Shows the crude, violent nature of Krusty's comedy - no sophistication

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: All you gotta do is stand there and get hit in the face with pies. It's simple!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's it? No jokes or anything?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Jokes? Kid, this is television! We don't need jokes, we just need pies!

jokes.text: I didn't do it.
jokes.setup: Bart accidentally knocks over expensive vase
jokes.punchline: Uses his new catchphrase in exactly the situation it was meant for
jokes.explanation: Smart callback that puts the catchphrase in its natural context - actual denial of guilt

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts!

jokes.text: Don't move, dude. This is totally a gun. I didn't do it.
jokes.setup: Armed robber threatens Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart responds with his catchphrase to actual crime
jokes.explanation: Shows how the catchphrase has become his automatic response to any accusation

dialogue.speaker: Robber
dialogue.line: Give me your money or I'll shoot!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts!

jokes.text: This is the third time that this building has burned down because someone has been smokin' in bed. I didn't do it. Help me!
jokes.setup: People trapped in burning building
jokes.punchline: Use Bart's catchphrase about deadly fire while asking for help
jokes.explanation: Dark humor showing catchphrase being used inappropriately in life-or-death situation

dialogue.speaker: Fire Victim
dialogue.line: Help! We're trapped in a burning building!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts!

jokes.text: This biography of Bart came out awfully quickly. It's not even about him. Sure it is. Look at the cover. But inside, it's mostly about Ross Perot and the last two chapters are excerpts from the Oliver North trial.
jokes.setup: Marge notes Bart's biography was published very quickly
jokes.punchline: It's completely unrelated content with only Bart's picture on cover
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of exploitative publishing that slaps celebrity names on unrelated content

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart's biography was published very quickly.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's completely unrelated content with only Bart's picture on the cover.

jokes.text: Ah, Oliver North. He was just poured into that uniform.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Oliver North trial excerpts in Bart's book
jokes.punchline: Homer's irrelevant attraction to North's military appearance
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer non sequitur focusing on appearance rather than substance

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. Oliver North? Isn't he that guy on TV?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes, dear. He was involved in that Iran-Contra scandal.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Iran-Contra? Sounds like a coffee drink. But look at that uniform! Those dress whites! That's a man who knows how to wear a uniform.

jokes.text: I have to pay to see my own grandson! That's the democrats for ya!
jokes.setup: Grandpa has to pay to see Bart perform
jokes.punchline: Blames Democrats for unrelated show business practices
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grandpa Simpson - blames political opponents for everything regardless of logic

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: Ten dollars just to watch my grandson perform? What's next, they'll charge me to breathe? This is what happens when you let the Democrats run show business!

jokes.text: See the boy-- five dollars. Or call him 24 hours a day on BartChat. Are you Bart? Sure, I am. I didn't do nothin'. Uh, isn't it, 'I didn't do it'? Yeah, whatever.
jokes.setup: Premium phone line to talk to 'Bart'
jokes.punchline: Obviously fake Bart who can't even get the catchphrase right
jokes.explanation: Good satire of celebrity exploitation with Barney's incompetent impersonation

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Hello? Bart?

dialogue.speaker: Fake Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, yeah, hey man. Eat my shorts... and also my socks.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: That's not right...

jokes.text: Come on, Lisa. Say somethin' funny. Like what? Oh, somethin' stupid like Bart would say. Bucka bucka, or woozle wozzle. Somethin' like that. Forget it, Dad. If I ever become famous I want it to be for something worthwhile, not because of some obnoxious fad.
jokes.setup: Homer wants Lisa to copy Bart's success with nonsense phrases
jokes.punchline: Lisa rejects fame based on stupidity, wants worthwhile achievement
jokes.explanation: Perfect character contrast - Lisa's integrity versus easy fame. Callback to earlier 'woozle wozzle'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, why can't you be more like Bart? He's famous for saying 'Eat my shorts' and 'Ay caramba!' You could be famous too!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, I don't want to be famous for saying stupid things. I want to achieve something worthwhile, something that actually means something.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Worthwhile? Lisa, Bart's making millions!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That doesn't make it right. I'd rather be known for my intelligence and my contributions to society.

jokes.text: Obnoxious fad? Ah, don't worry, Son. You know, they said the same thing about Urkel. That little snot boy. I'd like to smack that kid!
jokes.setup: Homer defends Bart's catchphrase by comparing to Urkel
jokes.punchline: Immediately expresses violent hatred for the character he just praised
jokes.explanation: Great self-aware moment about annoying TV catchphrases, with Homer's typical contradictory thinking

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, what's wrong with 'Eat my shorts'? Steve Urkel says 'Did I do that?' and everybody loves him.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'd like to kill that guy.

jokes.text: 'Just do the line.' 'Just do the line.' What's gonna happen to me?
jokes.setup: Bart repeats what everyone keeps telling him
jokes.punchline: Realizes he's trapped and questions his future
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's growing awareness of his predicament

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Everyone keeps telling me the same thing: 'Stay in school, Bart.' 'Apply yourself, Bart.' 'Stop mooning the principal, Bart.'

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... if I stay in school and apply myself, I'll graduate, go to college, get a job, get married, have kids, and then what? I'll just tell my kids the same things my parents told me?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm trapped in an endless cycle! Is this all there is?

jokes.text: And now, it's time for Match Game 2034 with Billy Crystal, Farrah Fawcett-Majors-O'Neal-Varney, the 'I didn't do it' boy, ventriloquist Loni Anderson, Spike Lee, and the always lovely and vivacious head of Kitty Carlisle.
jokes.setup: Bart dreams/fears his future in show business
jokes.punchline: Bizarre future game show with increasingly absurd celebrity names and disembodied head
jokes.explanation: Surreal nightmare vision of celebrity culture's future with perfectly escalating absurdity

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Welcome to 'Wheel of Questionable Choices,' the game show where celebrities spin a wheel to determine their fate!

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Our first contestant tonight is... Bort Sampson!

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Next up, we have... Chet Ubetcha!

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: And our third contestant is... a disembodied head in a jar!

dialogue.speaker: Disembodied Head
dialogue.line: Hello, I'm still alive!

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Welcome back to our show! Let's see what fate has in store for our guests today!

jokes.text: I didn't do it. I didn't do it.
jokes.setup: Bart performs his catchphrase on stage
jokes.punchline: Audience reaction is noticeably weaker, showing the fad is dying
jokes.explanation: Shows the inevitable decline of any overexposed entertainment phenomenon

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts!

dialogue.speaker: Audience
dialogue.line: *weak, scattered applause and laughter*

jokes.text: Woozle wozzle? What the-- That's what passes for entertainment these days? Woozle wozzle?
jokes.setup: Bart desperately tries another nonsense phrase when his catchphrase fails
jokes.punchline: Audience member dismisses it as bad entertainment, completing the failure
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to Homer's earlier bad advice, showing its failure in practice

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts!

dialogue.speaker: Audience Member
dialogue.line: That's not funny.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh... Eat my pants?

dialogue.speaker: Audience Member
dialogue.line: That's terrible.

jokes.text: What happened? Oh, don't worry about that. You're just finished, that's all. Finished? It happens all the time. That's show business for you. One day, you're the most important guy who ever lived. The next day, you're some schmo working in a box factory.
jokes.setup: Bart asks what happened to his career
jokes.punchline: Krusty casually explains the brutality of show business, mentions box factory
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback bringing the episode full circle - from box factory to fame and back

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What happened to your career?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Kid, show business is brutal. One day you're on top of the world, the next day you're working in a box factory.

jokes.text: I heard that! Boy, show business is kind of cruel, isn't it?
jokes.setup: Box factory worker overhears Krusty's comment
jokes.punchline: Bart understates the obvious cruelty he just experienced
jokes.explanation: Good understated conclusion with worker defending his workplace

dialogue.speaker: Krusty the Clown
dialogue.line: Hey, you got a problem with that?

dialogue.speaker: Box Factory Worker
dialogue.line: No, no problem at all, Mr. Krusty!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, that was a little harsh.

jokes.text: Say, speaking of one-trick ponies, whatever happened to that 'I didn't do it' kid? Boy, did that get old fast. Whoa! You know, if you wanna last in this business, you gotta stay fresh.
jokes.setup: Radio DJs discuss Bart's faded fame
jokes.punchline: Make obvious point about staying fresh while using tired sound effects
jokes.explanation: Smart irony - DJs criticize others for being stale while being stale themselves

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ 1
dialogue.line: You know, Bart Simpson used to be everywhere. Now? Nobody talks about him anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ 2
dialogue.line: That's right. The key to staying relevant is to constantly reinvent yourself and keep things fresh.

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ 1
dialogue.line: Absolutely. You can't just rely on the same old material.

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ 2
dialogue.line: Nope, gotta mix it up, stay current, keep the audience interested.

dialogue.speaker: Both
dialogue.line: *rimshot and horn sound effects*

jokes.text: You'll always have them to remind you of the time when you were the whole world's special little guy. Thanks, Mom. And now you can go back to just being you instead of a one-dimensional character with a silly catchphrase. D'oh! Ay, caramba! Hidilly-ho! Ha-ha! Excellent.
jokes.setup: Marge tells Bart he can stop being a one-dimensional character
jokes.punchline: Every other character immediately demonstrates their own one-dimensional catchphrases
jokes.explanation: Perfect meta-commentary that brilliantly exposes the show's own reliance on catchphrases - a masterpiece of self-aware writing

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, you can stop being a one-dimensional character.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Okily-dokily!

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: If you'll excuse me, I have a saxophone to practice.

jokes.text: What kind of catchphrase is that?
jokes.setup: Bart says a normal, non-catchy line
jokes.punchline: Homer doesn't understand communication without catchphrases
jokes.explanation: Perfect final button that extends the meta-joke - Homer can't process normal speech

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, can you help me with my homework?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eh? Did you say something? I wasn't listening. You gotta use catchphrases, boy. Like 'D'oh!' or 'Eat my shorts!' How else am I supposed to know what you're talking about?

tmdb_overview: When an on-air remark catapults Bart to instant stardom, he finds that fame isn't all it's cracked up to be.
insight: Absurdist chaos drives solid mid-tier episode with 2.59 jokes per minute across 57 gags.
